title: the aves at Union Arts Center (Partner Event)
description:
Union Arts Center in downtown Seattle is presenting performances of the aves on stage from April 18 - May 3, 2026.

About the play:
An older couple enjoys a summer day on a park bench, talking about birds and the likelihood of rain. A simple conversation…or maybe more. An ordinary afternoon transforms into a shifting world of surreal possibilities in a stunning and surprisingly funny meditation on memory, forgiveness, and the lifelong process of becoming who we are. From the mind of acclaimed playwright Jiehae Park in her signature innovative style, this Seattle premiere will reignite your sense of wonder.

Current Birds Connect Seattle members can use promo code BIRDS20 https://order.unionartscenter.org/overview/13053/?promo=BIRDS20 to receive 20% off single ticket purchases to all performances of Union Arts Center's the aves.

Learn more and purchase your tickets at: https://order.unionartscenter.org/overview/13053/?promo=BIRDS20 https://order.unionartscenter.org/overview/13053/?promo=BIRDS20
location: Union Arts Center,700 Union Street, Seattle, WA 98101

title: GiveBIG: The Future of Birds Starts Today.
description:
The future of birds starts today.

Right now across the Seattle region, baby birds are hatching and fledging in neighborhood trees, parks, shorelines, and wetlands. Their future depends on the choices we make today.

More than 220 species of resident and migratory birds call our region home. They pollinate plants, disperse seeds, control insects, and bring joy and inspiration to our daily lives.

But their future is uncertain. More than half of bird species are in decline, facing growing threats from habitat degradation and fragmentation, urban hazards like window collisions and pesticides, and a changing climate.

This GiveBIG, you can help change that.

Your gift today protects the places birds need to nest and raise their young, reduces threats in our cities, and powers the community science and advocacy that safeguard birds for generations to come.

Every gift made during GiveBIG helps to ensure the baby birds we celebrate this spring grow into thriving bird populations tomorrow.

GiveBIG for birds on or before May 5, 2026, to create a brighter future for birds.

Make Your Donation https://www.wagives.org/Birdsconnectsea https://www.wagives.org/Birdsconnectsea

title: BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class Series: Birds of the Central Puget Lowlands with Dr. Jack DeLap and Dr. Ursula Valdez
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu-central-puget-lowlands2026

The Central Puget Lowlands ecoregion embraces the lowlands and marine waters lying between the Cascades on the east and the coastal ranges on the west, from sea level to an elevation of about 1,000 ft. It contains Seattle and surrounding areas but also extends quite far to the west across Puget Sound. The original vegetation consists mainly of conifer-dominated forests of Douglas-fir, western hemlock, western red cedar, red alder, and bigleaf maple, but is now heavily fragmented due to human activity.

This is a two-part course series for intermediate to advanced birders that will be offered by local ornithologists and academics Dr. Jack DeLap and Dr. Ursula Valdez. Come learn with us about the lives of species found in the Central Puget lowlands. Some examples of the species to be covered: Bald Eagle, Barn Owl, Belted Kingfisher, several species of ducks and geese, Chestnut-backed Chickadees, Hummingbirds, Corvids, resident species of sparrows and finches, short and long-distance migrants.

Both instructors will give you the opportunity to learn about ID tips and the use of mobile apps for the documentation of the species.

Part A of this series will be taught by Dr. Jack DeLap. Topics covered include overwintering ecology and conservation, migration, and arrival..

Online Classes: meet weekly on Thursdays, April 2, 9, 16, 23 from 6:30 - 8:00 p.m.

Required field trips:

  1. Saturday, April 11 with Dr. DeLap, Location: Union Bay Natural Area / Montlake Fill, three-hour morning outing
  2. Saturday, April 25 with Dr. DeLap and Dr. Valdez, Location: Juanita Bay, three-hour morning outing

Part B of this series will be taught by Dr. Ursula Valdez and will focus on avian ecology and natural history associated with the establishment and defense of breeding territories, mating and reproduction, dispersal ecology, and conservation.

Online Classes: meet weekly on Thursdays, April 30, May 7, 14, 21 from 6:30-8:00pm

Required field trip:

  1. Saturday, May 16 with Dr. Valdez, Location: Carkeek Park, three-hour morning outing

We'll share more field trip information soon, including info about meeting locations and routes. Trip participants can expect to walk a leisurely 1-3 miles per outing, mostly on gently rolling dirt trails.

title: Community Speaker Series: How Sweet It is with Bryony Angell
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/howsweetitis-angell

Join us for our next Community Speaker Series event, "How Sweet It Is: Your love for coffee, chocolate and maple syrup can support bird conservation,” presented by Bryony Angell.

Did you know how you grocery shop can impact the conservation status of the migratory birds you love? Learn about the eco certifications from The Smithsonian Institute and Audubon Vermont which support wild birds habitats in the coffee, cocoa and maple syrup markets.

In addition to learning about these exciting products, attendees will get to enjoy samples of select chocolate and maple syrup as well as coffee tasting with local bird-friendly coffee roaster, Cloud City Coffee.

Bryony Angell writes and speaks about birding culture. Her stories focus on the human side of birding for resulting community connection and conservation impact. Her stories appear in Audubon. Org, the ABA’s Birding Magazine, BWD, The Seattle Times, and more. She’s been a guest speaker at Avistar Brazil, Cape May Spring Birding Festival, and local birding festivals across Washington State. She lives and birds in Skagit County. Read more of her content at www.bryonyangell.com http://www.bryonyangell.com/ .
location: Birds Connect Seattle headquarters and retail space (ground floor presentation area),616 Olive Wy, Seattle, WA 98101, USA

title: **Cancelled** BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class Series: Urban Birds with Dr. Jack DeLap and Dr. Ursula Valdez
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu-urban-birds2026

Online Classes: Tuesdays, April 7, 14, 28, and May 5, from 6:30 - 8:00 p.m.

Field Trips: Saturdays, April 18, May 2, and 9, 3 hours in the morning

Over 4 million people live in the urban corridor between Tacoma and Everett. This is the state's busiest transportation corridor, both land and water. Much of the original vegetation has been obliterated by land clearing, agriculture, logging, road building, and settlement. Dikes, dams, dredging, draining, filling, and reinforcement of stream banks and shorelines have greatly altered natural hydrology. Almost no old-growth conifer forest survives; the remnants are in tiny patches.

And yet, some bird species are flourishing. This module will cover the ecology and conservation strategies that enable birds to thrive in a place where most native habitats have been eliminated or heavily fragmented.

This course is for intermediate to advance birders interested in learning about the birds found in our backyards and urban environments. We will discuss ID tips for the most common urban birds, seasonal changes in species diversity and abundance, and natural history. This course will also cover the excitement and responsibility for bird feeder use, the main threats to urban birds (habitat reduction, cat predation, window, and car collisions), how to participate in documenting urban birds, and the impacts of birdwatching on human health.

We'll share more field trip information soon, including info about meeting locations and routes. Trip participants can expect to walk a leisurely 1-3 miles per outing, mostly on gently rolling dirt trails.

title: Partner Event: Birdability Blind Birder Bird-a-thon
description:
What is the Blind Birder Bird-a-Thon?

Participants who are blind or have low vision can bird anywhere in the world during a 24-hour window on May 3 or 4, 2026. Birding can take place in a backyard, park, balcony, accessible trail, or wherever listening to birds is possible.

Participants count the species they detect by sound or sight and report them to the Bird-a-Thon Compiler. There is no cost to participate. The focus is community, awareness, and access.

Full details and registration are available at https://www.birdability.org/blind-birdathon https://swn6onfbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001jWc-hVBAJ6yOaA5gssEwrR28zDCobyVgARXejIByTBv1XiAjn9pfQzckslDaoaDYn146SLCaxVVkW8abdi_LIw9whd6jjq0oRT6bvpnZIn6Rl_88tpd8XHyI4RmnZD9rb3CIYtUepeTnpUGIEVf3SMteBbRi_X3kcxreqwG0GaHzBf-s9XFR_g==&c=HaSaAy1kFmEV6dRKlJkPHnNXCHPSDmueoXkJQtGfqIDAAQpNQpMRcw==&ch=DP9PeYUqaZ0yPvMF70YbluN-MuPJN6YiPgn4kQFSZ7tKqW3hK4egUA==



How Individuals Can Participate

  * Birders who are blind or have low vision, and of any age and experience level, are invited to join. You can participate as an individual or as part of a team. You may bird alone, with friends, or with sighted volunteers.
  * Choose May 3 or May 4 and bird for as much of that 24-hour period as works for you. You can spread your birding time throughout the day or focus it all at once.
  * Bird anywhere that feels accessible and joyful. This might be your backyard, a favorite local park, an accessible trail, a balcony, a pond nearby, or a neighborhood sidewalk.
  * Count every species you detect by sound or sight during your 24-hour window. Use the tools that support you best, such as Merlin Bird ID https://swn6onfbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001jWc-hVBAJ6yOaA5gssEwrR28zDCobyVgARXejIByTBv1XiAjn9pfQ-7FtH7Rl16bExnGx732n-blgb0tXapvZntuW4YB9LrWCRl_QVW8DROyxd_H7VLX0TR9iOmacNhqPqjmMbB3eEeTYD9Q4GZIRPZRtQTutbVBNegH87OQzPcgCNBZjzLziBJnTD9PAzncP-SBItQo4c136iu7LPtqhrFl1zM2b9ViOptp-5-TsRavi9mgGUTNbsNbdwXrf7i0OhMdVbJgHwxIKmAC532mIXyLoXNJBeps&c=HaSaAy1kFmEV6dRKlJkPHnNXCHPSDmueoXkJQtGfqIDAAQpNQpMRcw==&ch=DP9PeYUqaZ0yPvMF70YbluN-MuPJN6YiPgn4kQFSZ7tKqW3hK4egUA== , recording devices, binoculars, guidebooks, or simply listening.

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Meadowbrook Pond Natural Area, Seattle
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

*Original date changed. Now on Sunday May 3rd*

Leader: Cynthia Putnam & Kathy Kaplan

Meeting Point: Meadowbrook Community Center, 10517 35th Ave NE, Seattle, WA

Bike Parking: Bike parking is in front of the community center.

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), C (sidewalks and hiking trails), approx. 1 mile walking

No mask requirements

Meadowbrook Pond is a wonderful little sanctuary which features diverse wildlife, interesting foliage, sculptures and trails all integrated so that humans and wildlife alike can enjoy the surroundings. It also plays a very practical role, capturing sediment from Thornton Creek and reducing downstream impact. The natural area features a wetland, wildlife habitat and open space. Bridges and walking platforms extend across the pond enabling visitors to get closer to the wildlife living in and visiting the pond. We hope to encounter a mix of passerines, raptors, and waterfowl. A significant portion of the walk lends itself to finding birds by their sounds, enabling us to practice our birding-by-ear skills. Our route will cover no more than 1 mile and usually takes 1.5 hours, depending on what we see and hear. Dress for weather; bring binoculars, water & snacks. Over by 10:30am.

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Sit, Sip, and Scope: Waterfront Birding in West Seattle
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Jordan Juranek (she/her) and Pedro Rodriguez (he/him)

Meeting Point: 9am at Charles Richey Sr Viewpoint 3521 Beach Dr. SW, Seattle, WA 98116

Bike Parking: Outdoor bike racks available

Trip Accessibility: 0, A if you stay on the paved pathway. 1, B if you are on the beach. Optional: 0.5 miles walking on the beach
This beach is characterized by large cobble, which is often covered in slippery algae. Someone with limited mobility would likely need to use a beach wheelchair to traverse this park, but you can also stay on the main pathway just above the beach. There is a well-maintained paved pathway that runs along the beach with a ramp and stairs leading down with beach access. The main pathway has an obstacle that would not be accessible by beach wheelchair. There is an easier entrance that is a 5-minute walk towards Alki Point with a ramp that has unobstructed access to the water.


Join NextGen Council Members Jordan & Pedro at Constellation Park & Charles Richey Sr. Viewpoint! This beach area lies on the southern side of Alki Point and offers beautiful views of the Puget Sound. Its main features are two large rock lines where you can find sea stars, anemones, barnacles, crabs and algae.

We will be using spotting scopes to look out at the water, and making our way down the beach front. Less than 0.5 miles walking along the beach or the paved pathway.

We'd recommend bringing waterproof shoes that you can walk along the beach in. Feel free to bring drinks & snacks to share, and chairs to sit in, as we will be mostly stationary and leisurely walking on the beach enjoying the view and using spotting scopes. Leaders will bring coffee & pastries.

Depending on the tide, here are some animals you might meet: Shore crabs, anemones, hermit crabs, ochre stars, gunnels, mussels, limpets, barnacles, many species of algae, great blue heron, sea lions, harbor seals, orcas, harbor porpoises, kelp crabs, red rock crabs, Dungeness crabs.

There is no parking lot at this site, but there is street parking and it is close to bus routes.

There are no public restrooms at this park. The closest bathrooms are located at Alki Beach, which is an approximately 7-minute car ride away. Bathrooms can be found on the main beach strip by Alki. However, the closest ones are located across the street from the Alki Beach location of the Cactus restaurant.

title: Winged Wonders: A Celebration of Birds and Conservation - Art Exhibit
description:
The Winged Wonders art show will celebrate local, up-and-coming or amateur artists who are inspired by birds. Birds Connect Seattle will display the art in our exhibit area for members of the community to experience, enjoy, and purchase.

Birds and nature inspire and inform in a unique and powerful way, and have attracted artists for centuries. From painters and photographers immersing themselves in the outdoors to capture the birds they're seeing, to musicians and filmmakers using audio and video of birds in their works, birdinspired art is everywhere.

Conservation can also inspire artistic expression. Recycling and reclamation of materials can add an additional layer of conservation messaging to a piece of bird-inspired art. Art, in turn, also inspires conservation. Powerful artistic images can lead viewers to action. It can spur conversations about emerging threats to birds and tells unique stories.

Exhibition Details and Key dates

All pieces on display will be available for purchase throughout the exhibition during Birds Connect Seattle's open hours (Tuesday – Saturday, 10:00 a.m. – 5:00 p.m.). 40% of sales will go to Birds Connect Seattle, and 60% paid by check to the artist. All pieces that are sold will remain on display through the end of the show and will be available for pick up by purchaser after June 1. Pieces will not be shipped.
location: Birds Connect Seattle headquarters and retail space (ground floor presentation area),616 Olive Wy, Seattle, WA 98101, USA

title: Online Class: Introduction to Flycatchers of the Pacific Northwest with Hans DeGrys
description:
Register here https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/flycatchers2026

Tuesday, May 5, 7:30-9:00pm, via Zoom

More than a dozen species of flycatchers migrate north to Washington state each spring to breed and raise their chicks. While flycatchers are notorious for being similar to each other and challenging to identify, most of our flycatcher species are actually quite distinct, with differing field marks, vocalizations, habits, and natural histories. This class will focus on flycatcher identification by sight, behavior, habitat, and voice. This session is suitable for beginning and intermediate birders.

title: BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class Series: Birds of the Columbia Plateau with Jason Fidorra
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu-columbia-plateau-2026

Online classes 6-7:30pm, Wednesdays, April 15, 22, 29, and May 6

Field trip May 16-17 based from Kennewick, WA

The Columbia Plateau ecoregion and it's shrubsteppe may challenge a visitor's expectations of the "evergreen state". While at times of year this treeless landscape can appear brown and lifeless, but an upclose visit in spring showcases abundant flora and fauna that make this habitat the most biodiverse terrestrial ecosystem in the Pacific Northwest!

This course consists of 4 online class sessions followed by a full 2-day weekend tour by local biologist Jason Fidorra. It covers the geographic history, ecology, and conservation status of the Columbia Plateau and shrubsteppe ecosystem, followed by in-depth focus on some of the avian specialties of the area: ferruginous hawk, burrowing owl, sage grouse, and more. The course also covers ecology and identification of many bird species present in the ecosystem and sought during the tour, many of which are unique to the ecosystem. Participants should complete the course with a deeper understanding of this part of Washington than is possible to gain on a single birding trip to the area and thus participants must have completed the course sessions to attend the field trip.

The weekend tour covers the southern Columbia Basin visiting Franklin, Benton, Walla Walla and Yakima Counties. Special effort will be made to search for rare and local species like ferruginous hawk and burrowing owls, in addition to visiting Shrubstepp habitats for Sage Thrasher, Loggerhead Shrike, Sagebrush Sparrow, Brewer's Sparrows and more. The trip includes wetland stops in hopes of breeding shorebirds (stilts, avocets, phalaropes) and riparian species (Lazuli bunting, eastern kingbird, bullock's oriole...) with the weekend trip list often exceeding 100 species!

title: Online Classes: Pelagic Birding
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/pelagicclass2026

Pelagic means “of the open sea,” and the west coast of North America offers some of the best opportunities to see seabirds anywhere in the world. Pelagic field trips out of Westport, Washington, have been offered since the late 1960s.

The rich waters 20-40 miles offshore support a diversity of birds —albatrosses, storm petrels, shearwaters, and jaegers—that spend much of the year in the waters far offshore at the edge of the continental shelf. If you’ve heard about pelagic birding and want to spend some time looking at those pages in your field guide you always skip over, this is the class for you.

The class lectures will cover the diversity of seabirds and marine mammals found offshore, focusing on identification and natural history, as well as tips on how to prepare for a pelagic trip. The instructor has been on more than 100 pelagic trips off the coast of Washington.

If you’re interested in going on a pelagic trip out of Westport, more information is available at: www.westportseabirds.com http://www.westportseabirds.com

Photo Credit: Black-footed Albatross, Mike Donahue

title: Field Trip:Oxbow Loop Trail in the Snoqualmie Middle Fork Valley near North Bend
description:
Register for the WAITLIST here https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/oxbow582026

Leaders: Cynthia Putnam and Dhammadinna Davis

Meet: 7:00 AM Green Lake (Ravenna) P&R. Northern section of the lot.

Limit: 12

No mask requirements

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly gravel trails, with some soft ground or mud), ~ 1.8 miles walking

In the Snoqualmie Middle Fork valley, we will walk a loop trail of 1.8 miles with 56 feet of elevation gain looking for spring arrivals. In early May our Puget Trough altitudinal migrants that spend the winter with us will be moving into the foothills and mountains. Varied thrushes, warblers, kinglets, and newly arrived flycatchers and vireos are likely. From a couple of vantage points, we will be looking down over the river or along the riverbank for waterfowl like Harlequin Ducks and Common Mergansers and American Dippers. At Oxbow Lake, we’ll be looking for Hooded Mergansers and other waterfowl that prefer calm waters.

The trail surface is gravel except for the offshoot to the riverbank. Vault toilets are at the trailhead. The gravel trail will not be muddy, but the side trail to the river most likely will be. We’ll spend about 2 to 3 hours in the field. Bring layers, rain gear, water and snacks. Return by 12 noon, depending on traffic.

title: Global Big Day 2026
description:

Our beloved annual Birdathon is back—now aligned with Global Big Day on May 9. Gather donations from friends, family, and coworkers, then head outside to see how many bird species you can find. It’s like a walk-a-thon, but instead of counting miles, we count birds, because when we count birds, we help save birds. Fundraise as an individual, then celebrate the day however you like: go birding with friends, join a group outing, or head out on your own for this fun and friendly birding challenge. Birders of all experience levels are welcome!

title: **Cancelled** BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class Series: Urban Birds with Dr. Jack DeLap and Dr. Ursula Valdez
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu-urban-birds2026

Online Classes: Tuesdays, April 7, 14, 28, and May 5, from 6:30 - 8:00 p.m.

Field Trips: Saturdays, April 18, May 2, and 9, 3 hours in the morning

Over 4 million people live in the urban corridor between Tacoma and Everett. This is the state's busiest transportation corridor, both land and water. Much of the original vegetation has been obliterated by land clearing, agriculture, logging, road building, and settlement. Dikes, dams, dredging, draining, filling, and reinforcement of stream banks and shorelines have greatly altered natural hydrology. Almost no old-growth conifer forest survives; the remnants are in tiny patches.

And yet, some bird species are flourishing. This module will cover the ecology and conservation strategies that enable birds to thrive in a place where most native habitats have been eliminated or heavily fragmented.

This course is for intermediate to advance birders interested in learning about the birds found in our backyards and urban environments. We will discuss ID tips for the most common urban birds, seasonal changes in species diversity and abundance, and natural history. This course will also cover the excitement and responsibility for bird feeder use, the main threats to urban birds (habitat reduction, cat predation, window, and car collisions), how to participate in documenting urban birds, and the impacts of birdwatching on human health.

We'll share more field trip information soon, including info about meeting locations and routes. Trip participants can expect to walk a leisurely 1-3 miles per outing, mostly on gently rolling dirt trails.

title: Online Class Series + Field Trips: In the Field Birding by Ear for Beginners with David Droppers
description:
Register here https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/beginners-birding-by-ear-2026

Field Trips: April 4th, April 19th, May 10th, May 24th, June 7th (participants are not expected to make every field trip, choose al la carte)

Have you ever marveled at the people who can hear a little tweet and know the identity of the speaker? Have you ever wondered just what these birds are talking about? Then this course is for you! Over several field trips and meetings, we'll practice the art/techniques of birding by ear, being able to identify birds without ever seeing them! We'll cover the challenges to birding by ear, and some of the tools that'll help. Between mnemonics, analogies, and practice, practice, practice, you'll be confident in your birding ear in no time!

We will meet for half-day trips from 7:00am-11:00am to practice what we've learned during the online classes. Plan to be on your feet for most of the trip, and walking perhaps a couple of miles, though the pace will be slow. The time between trips is to allow for different migrants to arrive, so we can experience and practice more bird calls, as well as get more practice with the resident species. Some dates have locations TBD; a poll will be taken from students to see if they want to return to the same location for all trips (to increase familiarity) or go to different areas on each trip (to increase exposure to different species).

title: Climate Watch Prospective Volunteer Info Session
description:
REGISTER HERE https://www.mobilize.us/birdsconnectsea/event/936662/

Since 2016, National Audubon’s Climate Watch community science program has been collecting data to determine how North American birds are responding to climate change.

This innovative community-science program enlists volunteer birders across North America to count certain target species twice each year. Beginner birders are welcome.

The data collected by volunteer scientists here in Seattle helps track whether birds are moving in accordance with projections from Audubon’s climate models. Target species for the Seattle area are Red-breasted Nuthatch and Spotted Towhee.

Join us for a free information session to learn about:

  * Survey routes and what a survey looks like
  * Time commitments and volunteer responsibilities
  * How to sign up for the upcoming summer survey season

title: Online Classes: Pelagic Birding
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/pelagicclass2026

Pelagic means “of the open sea,” and the west coast of North America offers some of the best opportunities to see seabirds anywhere in the world. Pelagic field trips out of Westport, Washington, have been offered since the late 1960s.

The rich waters 20-40 miles offshore support a diversity of birds —albatrosses, storm petrels, shearwaters, and jaegers—that spend much of the year in the waters far offshore at the edge of the continental shelf. If you’ve heard about pelagic birding and want to spend some time looking at those pages in your field guide you always skip over, this is the class for you.

The class lectures will cover the diversity of seabirds and marine mammals found offshore, focusing on identification and natural history, as well as tips on how to prepare for a pelagic trip. The instructor has been on more than 100 pelagic trips off the coast of Washington.

If you’re interested in going on a pelagic trip out of Westport, more information is available at: www.westportseabirds.com http://www.westportseabirds.com

Photo Credit: Black-footed Albatross, Mike Donahue

title: BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class Series: Birds of the Central Puget Lowlands with Dr. Jack DeLap and Dr. Ursula Valdez
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu-central-puget-lowlands2026

The Central Puget Lowlands ecoregion embraces the lowlands and marine waters lying between the Cascades on the east and the coastal ranges on the west, from sea level to an elevation of about 1,000 ft. It contains Seattle and surrounding areas but also extends quite far to the west across Puget Sound. The original vegetation consists mainly of conifer-dominated forests of Douglas-fir, western hemlock, western red cedar, red alder, and bigleaf maple, but is now heavily fragmented due to human activity.

This is a two-part course series for intermediate to advanced birders that will be offered by local ornithologists and academics Dr. Jack DeLap and Dr. Ursula Valdez. Come learn with us about the lives of species found in the Central Puget lowlands. Some examples of the species to be covered: Bald Eagle, Barn Owl, Belted Kingfisher, several species of ducks and geese, Chestnut-backed Chickadees, Hummingbirds, Corvids, resident species of sparrows and finches, short and long-distance migrants.

Both instructors will give you the opportunity to learn about ID tips and the use of mobile apps for the documentation of the species.

Part A of this series will be taught by Dr. Jack DeLap. Topics covered include overwintering ecology and conservation, migration, and arrival..

Online Classes: meet weekly on Thursdays, April 2, 9, 16, 23 from 6:30 - 8:00 p.m.

Required field trips:

  1. Saturday, April 11 with Dr. DeLap, Location: Union Bay Natural Area / Montlake Fill, three-hour morning outing
  2. Saturday, April 25 with Dr. DeLap and Dr. Valdez, Location: Juanita Bay, three-hour morning outing

Part B of this series will be taught by Dr. Ursula Valdez and will focus on avian ecology and natural history associated with the establishment and defense of breeding territories, mating and reproduction, dispersal ecology, and conservation.

Online Classes: meet weekly on Thursdays, April 30, May 7, 14, 21 from 6:30-8:00pm

Required field trip:

  1. Saturday, May 16 with Dr. Valdez, Location: Carkeek Park, three-hour morning outing

We'll share more field trip information soon, including info about meeting locations and routes. Trip participants can expect to walk a leisurely 1-3 miles per outing, mostly on gently rolling dirt trails.

title: BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class Series: Birds of the Columbia Plateau with Jason Fidorra
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu-columbia-plateau-2026

Online classes 6-7:30pm, Wednesdays, April 15, 22, 29, and May 6

Field trip May 16-17 based from Kennewick, WA

The Columbia Plateau ecoregion and it's shrubsteppe may challenge a visitor's expectations of the "evergreen state". While at times of year this treeless landscape can appear brown and lifeless, but an upclose visit in spring showcases abundant flora and fauna that make this habitat the most biodiverse terrestrial ecosystem in the Pacific Northwest!

This course consists of 4 online class sessions followed by a full 2-day weekend tour by local biologist Jason Fidorra. It covers the geographic history, ecology, and conservation status of the Columbia Plateau and shrubsteppe ecosystem, followed by in-depth focus on some of the avian specialties of the area: ferruginous hawk, burrowing owl, sage grouse, and more. The course also covers ecology and identification of many bird species present in the ecosystem and sought during the tour, many of which are unique to the ecosystem. Participants should complete the course with a deeper understanding of this part of Washington than is possible to gain on a single birding trip to the area and thus participants must have completed the course sessions to attend the field trip.

The weekend tour covers the southern Columbia Basin visiting Franklin, Benton, Walla Walla and Yakima Counties. Special effort will be made to search for rare and local species like ferruginous hawk and burrowing owls, in addition to visiting Shrubstepp habitats for Sage Thrasher, Loggerhead Shrike, Sagebrush Sparrow, Brewer's Sparrows and more. The trip includes wetland stops in hopes of breeding shorebirds (stilts, avocets, phalaropes) and riparian species (Lazuli bunting, eastern kingbird, bullock's oriole...) with the weekend trip list often exceeding 100 species!

title: Field Trip: Billy Frank Jr. Nisqually National Wildlife Refuge
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/nisquallymay17

Limit: 10

Billy Frank Jr. Nisqually National Wildlife Refuge includes a combination of estuaries, tide flats, freshwater marshes, woodlands, and grasslands. The Nisqually Delta is a major non-coastal resting and feeding area for migrating birds and there are few places in the region better for observing them. We will walk the forest and barn trails and go as far out on the boardwalk as we care to go. This event involves several miles of walking on good, flat trails with restroom opportunities at the start and mid-way. We’ll spend about 3-3.5 hours in the field. Pack snacks, lunch and water. Dress for wind and rain. Carpool costs: $84/car, shared equally by passengers. Bring layers, rain gear, water and snacks. Return 12:30 – 1:30PM, depending on traffic.

title: BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class Series: Birds of the Central Puget Lowlands with Dr. Jack DeLap and Dr. Ursula Valdez
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu-central-puget-lowlands2026

The Central Puget Lowlands ecoregion embraces the lowlands and marine waters lying between the Cascades on the east and the coastal ranges on the west, from sea level to an elevation of about 1,000 ft. It contains Seattle and surrounding areas but also extends quite far to the west across Puget Sound. The original vegetation consists mainly of conifer-dominated forests of Douglas-fir, western hemlock, western red cedar, red alder, and bigleaf maple, but is now heavily fragmented due to human activity.

This is a two-part course series for intermediate to advanced birders that will be offered by local ornithologists and academics Dr. Jack DeLap and Dr. Ursula Valdez. Come learn with us about the lives of species found in the Central Puget lowlands. Some examples of the species to be covered: Bald Eagle, Barn Owl, Belted Kingfisher, several species of ducks and geese, Chestnut-backed Chickadees, Hummingbirds, Corvids, resident species of sparrows and finches, short and long-distance migrants.

Both instructors will give you the opportunity to learn about ID tips and the use of mobile apps for the documentation of the species.

Part A of this series will be taught by Dr. Jack DeLap. Topics covered include overwintering ecology and conservation, migration, and arrival..

Online Classes: meet weekly on Thursdays, April 2, 9, 16, 23 from 6:30 - 8:00 p.m.

Required field trips:

  1. Saturday, April 11 with Dr. DeLap, Location: Union Bay Natural Area / Montlake Fill, three-hour morning outing
  2. Saturday, April 25 with Dr. DeLap and Dr. Valdez, Location: Juanita Bay, three-hour morning outing

Part B of this series will be taught by Dr. Ursula Valdez and will focus on avian ecology and natural history associated with the establishment and defense of breeding territories, mating and reproduction, dispersal ecology, and conservation.

Online Classes: meet weekly on Thursdays, April 30, May 7, 14, 21 from 6:30-8:00pm

Required field trip:

  1. Saturday, May 16 with Dr. Valdez, Location: Carkeek Park, three-hour morning outing

We'll share more field trip information soon, including info about meeting locations and routes. Trip participants can expect to walk a leisurely 1-3 miles per outing, mostly on gently rolling dirt trails.

title: NextGen Wingspan Game Night @ Old Stove Stove Ship Canal
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/nextgen-wingspan-game-night-may2026-oldstove

Date/Time: Thursday, May 21, 6:30-9 p.m.

Location: Old Stove Brewing Co - Ship Canal - 600 W Nickerson St, Seattle, WA 98119

Join Birds Connect Seattle's NextGen Council for an evening of board game fun! We have tables reserved for 40 attendees at Old Stove Ship Canal. The brewery has food (taco & beer food truck!) and beverages (beer, cocktails, wine, cider, and non-alcoholic drinks) for sale, but no outside food or drink allowed.

On the 3rd Thursday of the month, Old Stove (@old_stove_art_hop on Instagram) will also be hosting a local art show (including some bird art!) from 5-9pm, so be ready to come play Wingspan but also peruse the art show! Local creators and artists will gather to display (and sell) their work. Grab a pint and come hang out!

This location is family friendly, dogs are allowed. This event is hosted by NextGen, but gamers of all ages are welcome to register and attend. We will set up the board games and split into groups from ~6:30-6:45pm, and start playing right around 6:45/7pm so we wrap up by 9pm.

Wingspan is a massively popular, competitive, medium-weight, card-driven, engine-building board game from Stonemaier Games. It's designed by Elizabeth Hargrave and features over 170 birds illustrated by Beth Sobel, Natalia Rojas, Ana Maria Martinez, and Martha Clare. In the game (as in real life), you are bird enthusiasts—researchers, bird watchers, ornithologists, and collectors—seeking to discover and attract the best birds to your network of wildlife habitats. Each bird extends a chain of powerful combinations in one of your habitats (actions).

When you arrive, please look for the table tents with our event name on them (Birds Connect Seattle Board Game Night) or ask one of the friendly beertenders for assistance. Also, if you own the Wingspan board game, please bring it to the event, we will need some extra sets of the game and any expansion packs!

Please Note: Limited to 40 attendees. This event has limited slots, and you must register. If the event is full, please signup for the waitlist, and you will be moved up as people update their RSVPs.

title: Online Class Series + Field Trips: In the Field Birding by Ear for Beginners with David Droppers
description:
Register here https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/beginners-birding-by-ear-2026

Online Classes: Wednesday, April 1st and 8th from 7:00-8:30pm

Field Trips: April 4th, April 19th, May 10th, May 24th, June 7th (participants are not expected to make every field trip, choose al la carte)

Have you ever marveled at the people who can hear a little tweet and know the identity of the speaker? Have you ever wondered just what these birds are talking about? Then this course is for you! Over several field trips and meetings, we'll practice the art/techniques of birding by ear, being able to identify birds without ever seeing them! We'll cover the challenges to birding by ear, and some of the tools that'll help. Between mnemonics, analogies, and practice, practice, practice, you'll be confident in your birding ear in no time!

We will meet for half-day trips from 7:00am-11:00am to practice what we've learned during the online classes. Plan to be on your feet for most of the trip, and walking perhaps a couple of miles, though the pace will be slow. The time between trips is to allow for different migrants to arrive, so we can experience and practice more bird calls, as well as get more practice with the resident species. Some dates have locations TBD; a poll will be taken from students to see if they want to return to the same location for all trips (to increase familiarity) or go to different areas on each trip (to increase exposure to different species).

title: Online Lunch and Learn: Managing Seattle’s Urban Forest: Agencies, Plans, and Policies with Joshua Morris
description:
REGISTER HERE https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLScvoCDB95QMbi70oQV08ywuZSEG5_UZPwN5byVTH8wvijy7nw/viewform

This 15-minute presentation will provide a high-level overview of the public agencies responsible for managing Seattle’s urban forest, as well as the key plans, policies, and reports that guide urban forestry decisions. Fifteen minutes for Q & A will follow.

About the speaker: Joshua Morris is Conservation Director at Birds Connect Seattle. Joshua served on Seattle’s Urban Forestry Commission from 2019 to 2025 where he co-developed commission recommendations on updating tree protection ordinances, strengthening urban forestry policies in the City's Comprehensive Plan, improving tracking of urban forest expenditures in the City budget, and more.

This presentation is part of the Understory Lunch and Learn Series, an initiative of the Tree Equity Network https://www.seattleparksfoundation.org/community-partners/tree-equity-network/ offering accessible, concise information and resources on urban forest management, policy, and advocacy. Birds Connect Seattle and Seattle Parks Foundation co-lead the networks.

These include a nice mix of habitats - a pond in an open area, dense understory, wooded trails, and clearings adjacent to said trails. This could be a great place to check out what species may be migrating through during the spring, or potentially staying there for the summer. We'll start at Pond Park for a central meeting spot that should make potential parking spots closer to both the start and end points of the walk (the H, 5, and 21 bus lines also stop not far from it), and then walk through some of the Longfellow Creek trails. If there's time, we might also hit Greg Davis park or some Camp Long trails. Species we might look out for include a variety of swallow species (in particular over the pond area), flycatchers (Western Wood-Pewee, Western Flycatcher, maybe if we're lucky Olive-sided or Willow flycatchers), warblers, vireos, various birds of prey, ducks (on the pond), and of course our usual year-round species. title: Author Talk - Maria Mudd Ruth, The Bird With the Flaming Red Feet
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/mariamuddruth_flamingredfeet

All registrants to the talk will get a $5 off coupon to be used for purchase of the book. (Coupon code shared via confirmation email.)

Join us for an author talk with Maria Mudd Ruth, author of the upcoming book, Bird With the Flaming Red Feet, Seasons with an Uncommonly Common Seabird

The Bird with Flaming Red Feet is a lively and heartfelt natural history of the Pigeon Guillemot, a charismatic seabird with a surprising story to tell. Found along the coast around the North Pacific and from Alaska to Southern California, this talkative and often-overlooked bird plays an outsized role in the health of marine ecosystems, earning its status as an “indicator species.”

Author and longtime volunteer surveyor Maria Mudd Ruth weaves together science, field observation, and community storytelling to paint a vivid portrait of this special species and the ecosystems, both natural and human, surrounding it. Drawing from over a decade of beachside study, Ruth dives deep into the guillemot’s behaviors, quirky traits, and ecological relationships. Along the way, readers meet the passionate citizen scientists who have built a unique community around caring for and studying this vibrant bird.

Bridging the gap between field guide and memoir, The Bird with Flaming Red Feet invites readers to slow down, look closely, and reconnect with the wild places—and creatures—just outside their door. It’s a joyful call to observation, stewardship, and a deeper understanding of the natural world and our place in it.

MARIA MUDD RUTH has written more than a dozen nonfiction titles on natural history topics, including the critically acclaimed Rare Bird: Pursuing the Mystery of the Marbled Murrelet and A Sideways Look at Clouds. Ruth lives in Olympia, Washington, and has been part of a long-term community science study of the Pigeon Guillemots in the Salish Sea since 2013. To learn more, visit mariaruthbooks.net. http://mariaruthbooks.net.

description:

Meet: 6 AM Green Lake (Ravenna) P&R. South side of North lot near 65th St.

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly soft ground trails and boardwalk), ~3 miles walking

*NOTE: There will be TWO field trips meeting at the Park and Ride on June 6th at 6am. Please be sure that you connect with the correct group!*

Billy Frank Jr. Nisqually National Wildlife Refuge includes a combination of estuaries, tide flats, freshwater marshes, woodlands, and grasslands. The Nisqually Delta is a major non-coastal resting and feeding area for migrating birds and there are few places in the region better for observing them. We will walk the forest and barn trails and go as far out on the boardwalk as we care to go. This event involves several miles of walking on good, flat trails with restroom opportunities at the start and mid-way. We’ll spend about 3-3.5 hours in the field. Pack snacks, lunch and water. Dress for wind and rain. Carpool costs: $84/car, shared equally by passengers. Bring layers, rain gear, water and snacks. Return 12:30 – 1:30PM, depending on traffic.

title: Field Trip: Umtanum Creek
description:

The Umtanum Creek Trail offers a sample of the Eastside birds that we love to journey across the mountains to see. Early start is required because it is a 2-2 1/2 hour drive plus it can be warm later in the day. Golden Eagles, Bullock's Orioles, Yellow-breasted Chats, and Lazuli Buntings can be found while hiking this scenic valley. The trail starts in the beautiful Yakima River Canyon. This is a popular hike. There is a $5 charge per car unless you have a Federal Access Pass. Bring plenty of water for the hiking. Bring extra for the ride home as there is no potable water at the recreation area. Dress in layers for warm weather, bring sunscreen, trail snacks and a lunch for the end of the hike. We will hike about 4 miles total. The cost for carpooling will be split among the passengers at $0.70 per mile, which equates to about $175 per car. Please come prepared with cash to help. Here is a link with additional information by the Washington Trails Association (WTA): www.wta.org/go-hiking/hikes/umtanum-creek-canyon https://www.wta.org/go-hiking/hikes/umtanum-creek-canyon Back by 4pm.

title: Online Class Series + Field Trips: In the Field Birding by Ear for Beginners with David Droppers
description:
Register here https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/beginners-birding-by-ear-2026

Online Classes: Wednesday, April 1st and 8th from 7:00-8:30pm

Field Trips: April 4th, April 19th, May 10th, May 24th, June 7th (participants are not expected to make every field trip, choose al la carte)

Have you ever marveled at the people who can hear a little tweet and know the identity of the speaker? Have you ever wondered just what these birds are talking about? Then this course is for you! Over several field trips and meetings, we'll practice the art/techniques of birding by ear, being able to identify birds without ever seeing them! We'll cover the challenges to birding by ear, and some of the tools that'll help. Between mnemonics, analogies, and practice, practice, practice, you'll be confident in your birding ear in no time!

We will meet for half-day trips from 7:00am-11:00am to practice what we've learned during the online classes. Plan to be on your feet for most of the trip, and walking perhaps a couple of miles, though the pace will be slow. The time between trips is to allow for different migrants to arrive, so we can experience and practice more bird calls, as well as get more practice with the resident species. Some dates have locations TBD; a poll will be taken from students to see if they want to return to the same location for all trips (to increase familiarity) or go to different areas on each trip (to increase exposure to different species).

description:

Masks are optional

In Spring, our forests are green, buzzing with insects, and filled with the songs of passerines. Breeding/nesting activities are well underway, if not already completed. By June, we are near the end of spring and are often treated to baby birds, so we will definitely keep our eyes and ears open for them!

We will begin our June outing with a survey of the Puget Sound from the parking lot followed by a walk of Carkeek's beach to the mouth of Piper's Creek. We will then explore the park's rapidly-shifting wetland area, courtesy of Carkeek's resident master-contractors (i.e., beavers). Whatever the time of year, there are always interesting things to be seen and heard from the birds of Carkeek Park!


The walk will be slow-paced and leisurely, and may include some walking on dirt trails at a low to moderate incline. In Seattle, it is always possible that weather will be cool, windy, and/or rainy, so participants are encouraged to check the weather forecast prior to the walk and to dress/prepare accordingly (e.g., warm clothes, sturdy shoes, and rain gear for wet weather). The leader will have a pair of binoculars, a spotting scope, and a field guide. Participants are encouraged to bring their own binoculars, telescopes, and/or cameras if they have them.

title: Conservation Reading Group: A Natural History of Empty Lots by Christopher Brown
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/conservation-reading-group-a-natural-history-of-empty-lots

The Birds Connect Seattle Conservation Committee invites you to read and learn with us. Together, we'll deepen our understanding of conservation and become stronger advocates for birds and nature.

This quarter, we're reading A Natural History of Empty Lots: Field Notes from Urban Edgelands, Back Alleys, and Other Wild Places by Christopher Brown.

Purchase the book from our shop on bookshop.org https://bookshop.org/shop/birdsconnectsea.org , and the proceeds of the sale will support conservation and other programs at Birds Connect Seattle.

Make sure to follow along on Adabook https://www.addabook.net/book/9781643265223?bsid=120461&refu=birdsconnect&reflid=birdsconnect-L%3Ace309ad60c for prompts and reflections.

Book Description:

A Natural History of Empty Lots is a genre-defying work of nature writing, literary nonfiction, and memoir that explores what happens when nature and the city intersect. To do this, we must challenge our assumptions of nature itself.

During the real estate crash of the late 2000s, Christopher Brown purchased an empty lot in an industrial section of Austin, Texas. The property—a brownfield site bisected with an abandoned petroleum pipeline and littered with concrete debris and landfill trash—was an unlikely site for a home. Along with his son, Brown had explored similar empty lots around Austin, “ruined” spaces once used for agriculture and industry awaiting their redevelopment as Austin became a 21st-century boom town. He discovered them to be teeming with natural activity and embarked on a twenty-year project to live in and document such spaces. There, in our most damaged landscapes, he witnessed the remarkable resilience of wild nature, learned how easy it is to bring back the wild in our own backyards, and discovered that, by working to heal the wounds we have made on the Earth, we can also heal ourselves.

Beautifully written and philosophically hard-hitting, A Natural History of Empty Lots offers a new lens on human disruption and nature, offering a sense of hope among the edgelands.

Learn how the Conservation Reading Group works on our website https://birdsconnectsea.org/learn/conservation-reading-group/ .

title: NextGen Wingspan Night at Bizarre Brewing
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/nextgen-wingspan-game-night-june2026-bizarre

Date/Time: Wednesday. June 17th 6:30-9pm
Location: Bizarre Brewing 4441 26TH AVE W SEATTLE, WA 98199

Join Birds Connect Seattle's NextGen Council for an evening of board game fun, at one of our favorite breweries - Bizarre Brewing!

This event is hosted by NextGen, but gamers of all ages are welcome to register and attend. We will set up the board games and split into groups from ~6:30-6:45pm, and start playing right around 6:45/7pm so we wrap up by 9pm (when the brewery closes).


Wingspan is a massively popular, competitive, medium-weight, card-driven, engine-building board game from Stonemaier Games. It's designed by Elizabeth Hargrave and features over 170 birds illustrated by Beth Sobel, Natalia Rojas, Ana Maria Martinez, and Martha Clare.

In the game (as in real life), you are bird enthusiasts—researchers, bird watchers, ornithologists, and collectors—seeking to discover and attract the best birds to your network of wildlife habitats. Each bird extends a chain of powerful combinations in one of your habitats (actions). Beginners are welcome!

When you arrive, please look for the table tents with our event name on them (Birds Connect Seattle Board Game Night) or ask one of the friendly beertenders for assistance. If you own the Wingspan board game, please bring it to the event, we will need some extra sets of the game and any expansion packs!

Bizarre Brewing: The taproom is all ages and well-behaved, leashed dog friendly. The taproom has two all-gender ADA restrooms. Outside food is welcome. Bizarre also offers snacks for purchase including: Umami Kushi giant hot pretzel twist w/mustard, Damoori Kitchen pita chips + hummus, Bavarian Meats landjaeger, Beecher’s Cheese cheese sticks, Cougar Mountain cookies, and Tim's chips. Alcoholic and non-alcoholic drink options available. Ample street parking nearby.

Please Note: This event has limited slots, and you must RSVP. If the event is full, please signup for the waitlist, and you will be moved up as people update their RSVPs.
location: Bizarre Brewing,4441 26TH AVE W SEATTLE, WA 98199

Some pairs and trios of Pacific Northwest birds are difficult to distinguish by sound – at first! Such as American Robin and Black-headed Grosbeak. title: In-Person Author-Guided Waterfront Nature Walk with Kathryn True and Maria Dolan
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/seattlefieldguidewalk2026

The new Seattle waterfront provides plentiful delights for human visitors, including new bike and walking paths, inspiring artworks and wide-open views. But did you know that this stretch also includes new habitats and hiding places for Seattle's birds, animals and plants?

This walk is drawn from Seattle Field Guide, a new seasonal guidebook to urban nature outings by authors Kathryn True and Maria Dolan. On this one-mile, one-way walk along Seattle's transformed waterfront we'll look for birds, talk about how changes here enhance Chinook salmon migration (and how that affects our local orca populations), check out a kelp forest, and note the PNW artworks that tell natural and cultural stories along the route. End at Overlook Walk, at the entrance to Pike Place Market.

Meet at 199 Alaskan Way, in front of the Historic Washington Street Boat Landing Pergola, in front of Molly Moon's ice cream shop. This is a one way walk and ends at the Overlook Walk near the Seattle Aquarium. The walk will take about 60 minutes. Bring binoculars if you have them, but they aren't required.

Accessibility notes: Flat, paved sidewalk along waterfront. Habitat beach has rocks and sand but is viewable from the sidewalk. Step-free bathroom near the end of the walk.

About us:

Maria Dolan has written on birds, bats, orcas, environmental issues and other subjects for publications including Smithsonian.com, http://Smithsonian.com, CityLab, the Seattle Times, Sierra, Seattle Magazine and more. She lives in Seattle. Visit her online at mariadolan.org http://mariadolan.org or on Instagram bookish_md

Kathryn True embraces her naturalist tendencies through hiking, birding, community science, and swimming in cold mountain lakes. She blogs about the natural world for Vashon Nature Center and authored a Seattle Times series called "Natural Wanders." Her work has also appeared in Sunset and Alaska Airlines Magazine. She lives on Vashon Island, WA. Visit her online at kathryntrue.com http://kathryntrue.com or on Instagram @trueislander63

title: Field Trip: Big Four Ices Caves Trail
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/bigfourjuly2026

Leaders: Neil Zimmerman (he/him) (206 200 6700 day of only) and Roniq Bartanen (she/her)

6:00 AM Greenlake P&R, north side of 65th under I-5

Limit: 10

Trip accessibility: 1B (leisurely pace on soft ground, sand, gravel, dirt roads)

With its diverse habitat, the Big Four area is one of the best bird-watching locales in the North Cascades. The trail is unique because it allows birdwatchers to pass almost effortlessly from lowland marsh, meadow, and river habitats through classic forests, into the Alpine Zone. The birding can be exceptional, expect to see a variety of birds in the picnic and marsh area, including Red-breasted Sapsuckers, Vaux's Swifts, Willow Flycatchers, Common Yellowthroats, Yellow Warblers, and Warbling Vireos. American Dippers, Common Mergansers, Harlequin Ducks, and Spotted Sandpipers can be seen on the river. In recent years, this location has attracted breeding American Redstarts. This is one of the most westerly nesting locations in the country. Return to P&R at 4:00pm.

description:

Westcrest Park is the southern end of the West Duwamish Greenbelt and includes trails winding through mixed forest, a community garden, and open greenspace over the covered water reservoir. Anyone interested in birds is welcome – the trip leaders will have extra pairs of binoculars for folks who need them. Our route will first meander south through the open space and return via the trees to include different sorts of habitat. We’ll likely encounter Western Flycatchers, Bewick’s Wrens, Spotted Towhees, Red-breasted Nuthatches, Song Sparrows, American Robins. Perhaps Hutton’s Vireo. Pileated Woodpeckers, Cooper’s Hawks, and Common Ravens are always possible. A significant portion of the walk lends itself to finding birds by their sounds, because the tree canopy is high. Our route usually takes about 3 hours, depending on what we see and hear and how many questions spark conversation. End at 9:30 - 10:00 AM.

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: NextGen: Sammamish River Trail
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Please leave pets at home.

Meeting Point: We will meet directly outside the front doors of the Redmond Library (15990 NE 85th St, Redmond), the entrance facing the parking lot. There is a small plaza area there where everyone can gather comfortably before we head across the street to connect to the trail.

  * Trip Accessibility: 1A. Fully paved, flat, and level trail. Total distance will be roughly 1.5 to 2 miles walking at a very gentle, slow pace focused on mindful observation.
  * Parking: Plenty of free parking is available in the Redmond Library parking lot, including ADA-accessible spots.
  * Bathrooms: Restrooms are available inside the library during operating hours.


Join us for a Mindful Birding Walk along the Sammamish River Trail!

Slow down, engage your senses, and connect with the local environment on this gentle morning walk. Starting from the Redmond Library, we will quickly connect to the Sammamish River Trail, which offers a beautiful, open riparian habitat.


This walk is designed to be a mindful, slow-paced experience where we practice deep listening and observational awareness. The trail is completely paved and flat, making it highly accessible and a wonderful option for birders of all experience levels. Because this habitat blends river ecology with open brush and parkland, we will keep our eyes and ears open for resident and summer birds. We can expect to see or hear a variety of waterfowl and herons by the water, as well as songbirds like song sparrows, American goldfinches, cedar waxwings, black-capped chickadees, and potentially nesting swallows or raptors overhead.


Please bring binoculars if you have them, water, and comfortable walking shoes. No dogs, please, to help us keep a quiet and attentive presence for the birds

title: Author Talk with Pam McGaffin - Shade of Wings
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/pam_mcgaffin_shadeofwings

Date/Time: Thursday, July 16, 2026, 6:30pm

Location: Birds Connect Seattle HQ (616 Olive Way)

Join us for an author talk with Pam McGaffin, author of Shade of Wings, a speculative young adult novel about a family of New York City crows struggling to survive the outbreak of West Nile virus during the sizzling summer of 1999.

Four-year-old Duncan needs to hurry up and find a mate—or so says Cloud. But she doesn't know about the mistake that's preventing him from leaving their family to start another.

Though he's the eldest, Duncan doesn't see himself as a leader. Yet that's what he must become when both his parents die of the mysterious illness that's killing crows across New York City. He devotes himself to caring for his siblings, including three fledglings—but he soon discovers he can't protect them from the “blind death.”

Meanwhile, a zoo pathologist's worst fears are realized. It starts with dead flamingos. Then critically ill New Yorkers start showing up in hospital emergency rooms.

Some blame the crows.

Pam will be joined by Dr. Kaeli Swift, crow researcher and specialist from the University of Washington for a post-presentation discussion.

Pam McGaffin is a writer whose work has been published in literary journals, newspapers, and on websites. Her first novel, The Leaving Year, (SparkPress, 2018), won three finalist teen fiction awards and took first place in the 2018 Best Book Awards for young adult fiction. A proud bird nerd, she lives in Seattle, WA, with her husband, Mark, and rescue dog, Mars. On their daily walks, they are greeted by many crow friends.
location: Birds Connect Seattle headquarters and retail space (ground floor presentation area),616 Olive Wy, Seattle, WA 98101, USA

Leaders: Casey Herrick (she/her) and Jen Yu (she/her)

4:45am Marymoor Park Parking Lot G. 6046 West Lake Sammamish Parkway Northeast, Redmond, Washington 98052. $1 to park.

Limit: 10

Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (soft ground, dirt). We will walk at a leisurely pace on mostly flat terrain and cover a distance of around ~2 miles. There are bathrooms at the start and midway through our walk.

Join the NextGen Council as we lead a group through Marymoor in the early morning on a prowl for our local Seattle owls. We encourage you to bring a headlamp (with red light function if possible) for walking in the dark, and warm layers, with good hiking shoes. We will be birding at sunrise and it will be completely dark at the beginning of our walk, please bring headlamps or flashlights to ensure your safety while walking at night.

Our path will take us through an Audubon Bird Loop trail, which is mostly gravel roads with some paved and boardwalks as well. Our path will take us into the off leash dog park at Marymoor, so prepare to be in the presence of dogs. This walk is geared specifically for Young Adults under 40. Birders of all experience levels are welcome. Photography welcome, but no flash photography. End time is 7:00am.

description:

Group size will vary. Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions. Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Casey Herrick (she/her), Denning Gillespie (they/them) and Jules Stanley (she/her) - NextGen Council Members

Meeting Point: 9:00am - 11:00am, Juanita Bay Park (2201 Market Street, Kirkland, WA 98033). Meet in the parking lot off of Market St. Please note, this is not the same parking lot as Juanita Beach Park.

Bike Parking: No bike racks available at the park. There are small trees where one could secure a bike.
Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), A (mostly level and paved), ~ 2 miles walking. Restrooms at the start of our route.

Hello fellow Queer Birders! Juanita bay is one of my (Casey’s) favorite birding spots, and is considered one of the birding hotspots of the East side of Lake Washington. This park features views of Forbes Creek Wetland, Juanita Beach and Juanita Bay. It is a Green Kirkland Partnership restoration area. The maximum slope of the trail is moderate, with mostly flat walkways, such as the boardwalks.

We will be staying in relatively close proximity to the car park, with plenty of turn around points along the way. Bathrooms are easily accessible the whole trip.

This walk is geared specifically for anyone who identifies as LGBTQIA+ (Allies are of course welcome too!!) and young adults (under 40). Any level of birding experience is welcome.

title: Field Trip: Poo Poo Point Hike via SE Evans Lane
description:
Register directly with Issaquah Alps Trails Club (IATC) for this event:

https://givebutter.com/birds-connect-signup-poo-poo-point-birdwatching-hike-with-birds-connect https://givebutter.com/birds-connect-signup-poo-poo-point-birdwatching-hike-with-birds-connect

Leaders: Pedro Rodriguez 208-340-1703, Joel Gartenberg (Issaquah Alps Trails Club)

Meet: 6AM, SE Evans Lane Parking Area, Issaquah

Limit: 6 from BCS and 6 from Issaquah Alps Trails Club (IATC)

Trip Accessibility: Moderate pace on hiking trail with steep hills and significant elevation gain. Approximately 7 miles round trip with 2000ft elevation gain. Not a beginner hike; pace will accommodate varying fitness levels with time for stops and lunch.

Join Pedro Rodriguez (Birds Connect Seattle) and Joel Gartenberg (Issaquah Alps Trails Club) for an early morning hike to Poo Poo Point. Explore our local “Alps” and surround yourself with bird song. Joel frequently sees Western Tanagers, Flycatchers, Purple Finches, Orange Crowned Warblers and Wilson's Warblers, Varied Thrushes, Rufous Hummingbirds, Pileated and Hairy Woodpeckers. Experience a less crowded and less steep alternative to the main Poo-Poo Point trail, allowing time for stops and snacks. End time at 11AM based on group's preference and pacing.

What to Bring: Minimum 1.5L of water (temps can be very hot), lunch/snacks, layers, sun protection, and sturdy shoes. Binoculars are recommended for bird watching and limited quantities can be loaned (ideally with advance notice). No parking pass required.

Hike: https://maps.app.goo.gl/uuy6DVe1cfKi5J2G7 https://maps.app.goo.gl/uuy6DVe1cfKi5J2G7

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Birds and Habitat – Along Arboretum Creek
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leader: Larry Hubbell (he/him)

Meeting Point: The Birch Parking Lot – on the east side of Lake Washington Blvd. This is the only parking lot that is north of the Japanese Garden and south of the Boyer Ave / Lake Washington Blvd intersection.

Bike Parking: Yes. There is a bike rack.

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly gravel trails with some soft ground or mud)

We walk north approximately 1.5 miles through the Arboretum, often next to or parallel with Arboretum Creek and usually end up near Duck Bay (south of Foster Island). We will be searching for birds and discussing their habitat choices for food, nesting and roosting. We begin at 8:30 am and the trip will end around 10:30 am near Duck Bay – which is about one mile north of the Birch Parking Lot. However, walking south, back to your vehicle, may take another 20-30 minutes depending on each individual’s motivation. To learn more about our efforts to enhance the Arboretum Creek habitat for birds and nature please visit: www.arboretumcreek.org https://www.arboretumcreek.org

Photo Credit: Rod Fung

title: Online Class Series + Optional Field Trip: Shorebirds with David Droppers
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/shorebirdsdroppers2026

Shorebirds are not popular with beginning birders because they're so similar, so variable, and such a challenge. Shorebirds are popular with experts for the exact same reasons! So don't be intimidated by these fascinating, and frankly cute, group of birds that grace our shores (and other habitats) for brief periods of the year. We'll focus on identification, but more so, teach you tricks that willl make tackling shorebirds much less intimidating. A field trip to the coast during shorebird migration will help solidify what we learn in class. Move from beginner to expert so you can get excited about shorebird season!

FIELD TRIP: We'll travel to the coast, seeking different habitats to find different shorebirds. Our main event will be Bottle Beach as the tide comes in, but we'll also visit marinas, rocky dikes, and other locations from Bottle Beach to Tokeland. There will be an announcement a couple of days before the trip outlining details, particularly forecast, but plan to bring a lunch and be out for most of the day. We'll meet at the Green Lake Park n Ride at 6:30 AM and carpool from there. Drivers should have a Discover Pass for parking at Bottle Beach.

Online Classes: Thursdays, August 13, 20, and 27 from 7-8:30pm

Field Trip: Saturday, August 29 to Westport (Bottle Beach, Grayland Beach, Tokeland)

title: BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class - Bird by Ear: Laying the Foundation for a Lifetime of Listening with Whitney Neufeld-Kaiser
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu_bbe2026

Online Classes: Tuesdays, September 1 and 8, from 6:30 - 8:30pm

Field Trip: Sunday, September 13, from 7-9:00 AM - location TBD

Bird by Ear: Laying the Foundation for a Lifetime of Listening

Identifying birds by their songs and calls can transform both your everyday life and your birding experience. It's also an integral part of Bird University. But how do you get started on this project, which may last your lifetime? This 2-part class will cover the foundations of active, analytical listening and introduce you to resources and techniques for learning how to identify birds by ear. The optional field trip will give you a chance to practice what you've learned.

Learn more about instructor Whitney https://birdsconnectsea.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Whitney-Neufeld-Kaiser.pdf

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly gravel trails with some soft ground or mud)

We walk north approximately 1.5 miles through the Arboretum, often next to or parallel with Arboretum Creek and usually end up near Duck Bay (south of Foster Island). We will be searching for birds and discussing their habitat choices for food, nesting and roosting. We begin at 8:30 am and the trip will end around 10:30 am near Duck Bay – which is about one mile north of the Birch Parking Lot. However, walking south, back to your vehicle, may take another 20-30 minutes depending on each individual’s motivation. To learn more about our efforts to enhance the Arboretum Creek habitat for birds and nature please visit: www.arboretumcreek.org https://www.arboretumcreek.org

Photo Credit: Rod Fung
location: Birch Parking lot, Washington Park Arboretum

title: BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class - Bird by Ear: Laying the Foundation for a Lifetime of Listening with Whitney Neufeld-Kaiser
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu_bbe2026

Online Classes: Tuesdays, September 1 and 8, from 6:30 - 8:30pm

Field Trip: Sunday, September 13, from 7-9:00 AM - location TBD

Bird by Ear: Laying the Foundation for a Lifetime of Listening

Identifying birds by their songs and calls can transform both your everyday life and your birding experience. It's also an integral part of Bird University. But how do you get started on this project, which may last your lifetime? This 2-part class will cover the foundations of active, analytical listening and introduce you to resources and techniques for learning how to identify birds by ear. The optional field trip will give you a chance to practice what you've learned.

Learn more about instructor Whitney https://birdsconnectsea.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Whitney-Neufeld-Kaiser.pdf

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: LGBTQIA+ Birders at Marymoor Park (for young adults <40)
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outing: LGBTQIA+ Birders at Marymoor Park (for young adults <40)

Group size will vary. Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions. Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Casey Herrick (she/her), Denning Gillespie (they/them) and Jules Stanley (she/her) - NextGen Council Members

Meeting Point: 9:00am. Marymoor Park Parking Lot G. 6046 West Lake Sammamish Parkway Northeast, Redmond, Washington 98052. $1 to park.

Bike Parking: There are Heron shaped bike racks at the start of our route!


Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (soft ground, dirt). We will walk at a leisurely pace on mostly flat terrain and cover a distance of around ~2 miles. There are bathrooms at the start and midway through our walk.

Hello fellow Queer Birders! We will be birding this time at Marymoor Park.Considered one of the birding hotspots of the East side, Marymoor Park features views of Lake Sammamish, the Sammamish river trail, and a diverse population of birds. Our path will take us through an Audubon Bird Loop trail, which is mostly gravel roads with some paved and boardwalks as well. Our path will take us into the off leash dog park at Marymoor, so prepare to be in the presence of dogs.

This walk is geared specifically for anyone who identifies as LGBTQIA+ (Allies are of course welcome too!!) and young adults (under 40). Any level of birding experience is welcome.
location: Marymoor Park,6046 West LK Sammamish Pkwy NE, Redmond, WA 98052, USA

title: Children's Neighborhood Bird Outing: Seward Park
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leader: Erin Connelly (she/her)

Meeting Point: 9:30 AM Seward Park Audubon Center, near the bathrooms

Bike Parking: bike parking adjacent to the Audubon Center; other transportation info: Parking lots and street parking available (although it can be busy on weekends!). Seward Park is served by bus route 50 with a stop at Seward Park Ave S and S Juneau St.

Accessibility: Pace: 1, Terrain: A, Walking: 1/2 - 1 miles
Wheelchair accessible, as well as strollers, kid bikes, scooters etc. We will be walking on flat paved trails, likely walking between 1/2 - 3/4 mile with lots of stops, walking at the children's pace. Public bathrooms available at the Audubon Center.

What to Expect: Open to all babies, toddlers, and kids. All children must be accompanied by an adult. We will gather at the Audubon Center and walk north and east towards the swimming beach on the paved perimeter trail (Shore Loop Road). We will practice observing birds with our eyes and ears and look for mallard ducks, Canada geese, buffleheads, crows, chickadees, juncos, and more on a short walk.

We will take a snack, story, and/or look-at-interesting-rocks break somewhere in the middle depending on the pacing and interest level of the kids. Please no adults not accompanied by a child.
location: Seward Park Nature Center,5902 Lake Washington Blvd S, Seattle, WA 98118, USA

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Union Bay Natural Area
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Julia Hansbrough & Dick Holcomb & Shelly Evans

Meeting Point: Center for Urban Horticulture in the East parking lot off NE 41st Street, one block beyond the place where Mary Gates Memorial Drive turns left to become NE 41st Street

Bike Parking: Yes; bike rack in back of building

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly gravel trails, with some soft ground or mud), ~1.5 miles walking

Union Bay Natural Area, just east of the University of Washington, offers a range of habitats: wetland to forest to open fields. Likely birds this time of year include wood duck, savannah sparrow, and osprey, possible birds include common yellowthroat, marsh wren, and Wilson's warbler.
location: Union Bay Natural Area,3501 NE 41st St, Seattle, WA 98195, USA

title: Community Speaker Series - Birdbrains Book Talk with Susan Rich, Stephanie Delaney, and others
description:
Registration coming soon

Date: Thursday, September 24, 6:30om

Location: Birds Connect Seattle (616 Olive Way)

Birdbrains: A Lyrical Guide to Washington State Birds is a one-of-a-kind Washington State bird guide. The anthology includes original pen and ink sumi-e paintings by artist Hiroko Seki, bird fact notes, and short poems/stories/memoir. Poems/prose are bird-focused rather than human centered. The bird notes include information about song, call, mating rituals, habitat, migration, climate, dark comedy, and fun facts.

Join us for an evening of conversation and poetry from several contributors to this anthology, including editor Susan Rich, bird bio author Stephanie Delaney, and several contributing poets.
location: Birds Connect Seattle headquarters and retail space (ground floor presentation area),616 Olive Wy, Seattle, WA 98101, USA
